prompt:
Studio tech manager here. Our recording rooms share one rack of outboard gear and the sign-out situation is a whiteboard that nobody erases. Sessions walk off with the 1176 for a week, mixdowns stall, and when I ask who has it the whiteboard says three different answers. I want the booking logic in code — a lease board with real using-block discipline. C# / .NET 10, namespace PatchBay, one project. GearBoardTests.cs is the contract; csproj and .gitignore stay as they are.

Shape I want:

class GearBoard, constructed from the rack inventory (params string[] units). A duplicate name in the inventory is a setup mistake: ArgumentException, message starting with "duplicate unit '<name>'".
- GearLease Acquire(string unit, string session) — checks the unit out to that session. Unknown unit: KeyNotFoundException "no such unit '<unit>'". Already out: InvalidOperationException "unit '<unit>' is already leased to session '<session-that-has-it>'" — that exact sentence, it goes straight on the studio slack.
- int ActiveCount and IReadOnlyList<string> ActiveUnits — what's currently out, ActiveUnits sorted ordinally (StringComparer.Ordinal — the rack has mixed-case unit names and I want one stable order, not culture roulette).
- IReadOnlyList<string> ReleaseLog — every release ever, oldest first, entries formatted "<unit>:<session>".
- void AssertAllReleased() — end-of-day check. Everything back: returns quietly. Anything still out: InvalidOperationException "unreleased units: <a>, <b>" with the names ordinal-sorted, comma-space separated.

sealed class GearLease : IDisposable — the thing a session holds. Unit and Session string properties, bool IsReleased. Dispose returns the unit to the board EXACTLY once — a second or tenth Dispose is a silent no-op, never a double release-log entry, never a throw (using blocks and catch blocks both dispose in our helpers, so double-dispose is normal life). string Route(string channel) patches the unit into a console channel and returns "<unit><-<channel>"; on a released lease that's an ObjectDisposedException whose ObjectName is the type name GearLease. And I am firm about this after getting burned by a 'clever' wrapper years ago: NO finalizer on the lease type — cleanup is deterministic disposal, full stop. One of the tests reflects for a Finalize override and will fail the PR if one appears.

Everything single-threaded, no timers, no GC games. `dotnet test` green = ship it.
